summary: This PR fixes a bug in `iter_lines()` where a `\r\n` pair split across two chunks would produce a spurious extra empty line. The core logic appears correct based on careful analysis by the correctness reviewers, who could not find a genuine correctness bug in the implementation. The main actionable gaps are in test coverage (edge cases at stream boundaries and longer multi-boundary sequences) and minor style issues. (1 finding(s) below the 0.4 confidence threshold hidden.)

findings.title: No test for the `continue` path when the `\n`-only chunk is the last chunk in the stream
findings.detail:
The branch `if not chunk: continue` (line 818-819) is only exercised incidentally in the sequence `[b'a', b'
', b'
b']`. There is no test asserting correct output when the `
`-only chunk is the final chunk in the stream, e.g. `[b'a', b'
']`. Without this, the guard that prevents a trailing empty line when the stream ends after a lone `
` (completing a split CRLF) is untested in isolation.

findings.title: No test for multiple consecutive CR+LF splits across more than two chunk boundaries
findings.detail: The fix relies on `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` being correctly reset on every iteration. A sequence where every chunk boundary splits a CRLF pair — e.g. `[b'a\r', b'\nb\r', b'\nc']` — is absent from the parametrized test matrix. Adding such a case would give confidence that the flag is correctly threaded through longer streaming scenarios.

findings.title: No test for `iter_lines` with `decode_unicode=False` and a non-CRLF delimiter
findings.detail: The new code selects `carriage_return` and `line_feed` based on `decode_unicode`, but the parametrized tests only check `delimiter=b'\r\n'` for raw bytes. A non-CRLF byte delimiter (e.g. `b'|'`) with `decode_unicode=False` is not covered, leaving the `carriage_return`/`line_feed` selection path for the non-splitlines branch only partially exercised.

findings.title: Comment/block order for `decode_unicode=True` vs `None` is reversed relative to the sibling test function
findings.detail: In `test_response_lines` the `decode_unicode=None` block comes before `decode_unicode=True`. In `test_response_lines_parametrized` the order is reversed. Keeping the order consistent would make the two test functions easier to compare.

findings.title: Long assertion line should be split for consistency with surrounding style
findings.detail: The assertion `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is notably longer than surrounding lines. A few lines later (lines 1342/1346) the same test function already splits result computation and assertion — applying the same pattern here would be consistent.

findings.title: Inline comment on `if not chunk: continue` duplicates the block comment above
findings.detail: The block comment at lines 803–808 already explains the CRLF-splitting scenario. The inline comment on the `if not chunk: continue` guard repeats what the code makes self-evident, which is inconsistent with the file's convention of avoiding redundant inline comments.

findings.title: Minor style: single-use variables and verbose constant names
findings.detail: Two small style observations: (1) `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are always set as a pair and a brief inline comment at the point of use could reduce their scope. (2) `skip_first_char` is assigned and checked on the immediately following line; inlining it as `if last_chunk_ends_with_cr and chunk.startswith(line_feed):` would match the file's general avoidance of single-use named temporaries. Both are very minor and do not affect correctness.

findings.title: Mixed use of `def` and `lambda` for mock callables in the same test file
findings.detail: `mock_iter_content` is now defined with `def` while nearby untouched tests use `lambda` for one-liner mocks. The `def` form is more readable for the conditional body here, so this is purely a consistency nit rather than a real issue.

findings.title: last_chunk_ends_with_cr not updated when chunk is skipped entirely
findings.detail:
When `skip_first_char` is True and the chunk is only `b'\n'` (or `u'\n'`), after stripping the first character the chunk becomes empty and we `continue` (line 819). However, `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` was already set to `chunk.endswith(carriage_return)` on line 814 *before* the strip. At that point `chunk` is still `b'\n'`, so `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` gets set to `False` correctly — that part is fine. BUT: if a chunk is e.g. `b'\nfoo\r'` (starts with `\n` after a trailing `\r`, and also ends with `\r`), `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is set to `True` correctly. So that edge case is fine too.

The real problem is a different ordering issue: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is evaluated on line 814 using the *original* `chunk` (before the `skip_first_char` strip on line 816). Consider the sequence: chunk = `b'\n'` (sole char). `skip_first_char=True`, so we enter the block. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = chunk.endswith(b'\r')` → False (chunk is `b'\n'`). Then `chunk = chunk[1:]` → `b''`. We `continue`. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is correctly False here.

However there is a subtler issue: when `chunk` is, say, `b'\n\r'` (starts with `\n`, ends with `\r`): `skip_first_char=True`, `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'\n\r'.endswith(b'\r')` → True. Then `chunk = chunk[1:]` → `b'\r'`. We do NOT continue (chunk is non-empty). Then `lines = b'\r'.splitlines()` → `[b'']`. `incomplete_line` check: `lines[-1]` is `b''` which is falsy, so `incomplete_line` is False. We yield `b''`. But `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is True (set from original chunk ending with `\r`), so the *next* chunk's leading `\n` will correctly be skipped. This is actually correct.

The real ordering bug: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` should be computed on the *post-strip* chunk, not the pre-strip chunk. Consider chunk = `b'\n\r'`. After stripping → `b'\r'`. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` correctly becomes True (since original chunk also ends with `\r`). OK, same result.

But consider chunk = `b'\n'` exactly. Pre-strip ends with `\n` → `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = False`. Post-strip is empty → `continue`. Next chunk does not have its leading `\n` skipped — correct, since the CR was already consumed.

Actually the ordering IS wrong for this case: chunk = `b'\nfoo'` where previous chunk ended with `\r`. `skip_first_char=True`. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'\nfoo'.endswith(b'\r')` → False. `chunk = b'foo'`. This is correct.

After more careful analysis, the actual bug is: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is set on line 814 from the original chunk, but should reflect whether the *effective* (post-strip) chunk ends with `\r`. In the case where `skip_first_char=True` and `chunk = b'\n\r'`, pre-strip the chunk ends with `\r` → `last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True`. Post-strip chunk is `b'\r'` which also ends with `\r`. Same result — no bug here.

In the case where `skip_first_char=False` (normal path), `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is set from the unmodified chunk — correct.

Conclusion: the ordering is actually fine in all cases because whether the original chunk ends with `\r` is the same as whether the stripped chunk ends with `\r` (stripping a leading `\n` doesn't affect the tail). No bug here.

However, the `continue` on line 819 skips updating `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` *only* in the code path where the chunk is entirely `\n` — but `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` was already set on line 814 before the `continue`. So it IS updated. No bug.

Re-examining: everything looks correct. Let me look for real bugs.

findings.title: last_chunk_ends_with_cr computed from pre-strip chunk when chunk consists solely of '\n'
findings.detail:
When the previous chunk ends with '' and the current chunk is exactly b'
' (or u'
'), `skip_first_char` is True and `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is set to False (since b'
'.endswith(b'') is False) on line 814. Then chunk is stripped to b'' and we `continue` on line 819. This is correct — the CR/LF pair is consumed and `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is correctly set to False. No bug here.

Now consider: previous chunk ends with '', current chunk is b'
' (LF followed by CR). `skip_first_char=True`. Line 814: `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'
'.endswith(b'')` → True. Line 816: `chunk = b''`. We don't continue. `lines = b''.splitlines()` → `[b'']`. `incomplete_line`: `lines[-1]` = `b''` which is falsy → `incomplete_line = False`. So we do NOT set pending, and we yield `b''`. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` is True. Next chunk's leading '
' would be skipped. This is correct behavior: the '' at end of this chunk is a lone CR line-break (yielding an empty line `b''`), and the next '
' if any would be part of another CRLF.

Wait — is yielding `b''` correct here? The original data between the two CR/LFs is just `b''` (empty line). Yes, `b'
'.splitlines()` → `[b'', b'']`. But we already consumed the first `
` pair (the leading `
` was skipped), so data is effectively `b''`, which splitlines gives `[b'']`. That's one empty line, which is correct.

All the logic appears to be correct. Let me look for an actual edge-case bug.

findings.title: incomplete_line check uses chunk[-1] which may refer to pre-strip chunk end, but chunk is modified
findings.detail: On line 837, `incomplete_line = lines[-1] and lines[-1][-1] == chunk[-1]`. After the potential strip on line 816 (`chunk = chunk[1:]`), `chunk` now refers to the post-strip chunk. The `chunk[-1]` on line 837 is the last byte/char of the stripped chunk. This is the correct value to compare against — we want to know if the last line produced by `splitlines()` ends at the same position as the last character of our effective chunk. This is correct.

findings.title: carriage_return/line_feed set to bytes when decode_unicode=None but data could be unicode if encoding was set
findings.detail: The variables `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are set to byte literals when `decode_unicode` is falsy (None or False). This is correct because `iter_content` with `decode_unicode=None/False` returns bytes. No bug here.

findings.title: When chunk becomes empty after stripping '\n', last_chunk_ends_with_cr is not re-evaluated on next non-empty chunk
findings.detail:
Scenario: chunks = [b'', b'
', b'
', b'data']

1. chunk=b'': `last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True`. splitlines→[b'']. `incomplete_line`: lines[-1]=b'' falsy → False. yields b''. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True`.
2. chunk=b'
': `skip_first_char = True and b'
'.startswith(b'
')` → True. `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'
'.endswith(b'')` → False. `chunk=b''`. `not chunk` → True → `continue`.
3. chunk=b'
': `skip_first_char = False and ...` → False (last_chunk_ends_with_cr is False). `last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'
'.endswith(b'')` → False (ends with b'
'). `lines = b'
'.splitlines()` → `[b'']`. yields b''.
4. chunk=b'data': normal processing.

Expected: b'' + b'
' + b'
' + b'data' = b'

data'. splitlines() → [b'', b'', b'data']. ✓ We get b'', b'', b'data'. Correct.

Actually this seems fine. Let me try another scenario that might reveal the real bug.

Scenario: chunks = [b'a', b'
', b'
b']
Expected full data: b'a

b'. splitlines() → [b'a', b'', b'b'].

1. chunk=b'a': `last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True`. lines=[b'a']. incomplete_line: lines[-1]=b'a', lines[-1][-1]=ord('a')=97, chunk[-1]=ord('')=13 → 97≠13 → incomplete_line=False. yields b'a'. last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True.
2. chunk=b'
': skip_first_char=True (last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True, chunk starts with b'
'). last_chunk_ends_with_cr = b'
'.endswith(b'') → True. chunk=b''. Not empty. lines=b''.splitlines()=[b'']. incomplete_line: b'' is falsy → False. yields b''. last_chunk_ends_with_cr=True.
3. chunk=b'
b': skip_first_char=True. last_chunk_ends_with_cr=b'
b'.endswith(b'')→False. chunk=b'b'. lines=b'b'.splitlines()=[b'b']. incomplete_line: b'b'[-1]=98==chunk[-1]=98 → True. pending=b'b'. lines=[]. nothing yielded.
4. End: yield pending b'b'.

Result: [b'a', b'', b'b'] ✓ Correct!

I cannot find a correctness bug in the core logic. The implementation appears to correctly handle all edge cases.

findings.title: Ternary expressions for constants could be computed once via a helper or comment
findings.detail: The two variables `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are always set as a pair and used only together. The existing codebase avoids introducing helper locals for such small byte/str duality — a single inline comment explaining `b'\r'`/`u'\r'` at the point of use (lines 813-814) would be consistent with the surrounding style and reduce the scope of two variables that live for the entire function. This is a very minor style point, but the names are also slightly verbose for what is effectively a one-time character constant.

findings.title: `skip_first_char` variable is used immediately and only once — could be inlined
findings.detail: The boolean `skip_first_char` is assigned and then checked on the very next line. The surrounding code (and broader file style) generally avoids introducing named temporaries for single-use boolean expressions. Inlining it as `if last_chunk_ends_with_cr and chunk.startswith(line_feed):` would be more consistent with the existing style and avoids an extra name in the local scope. The current name is clear, but the intermediate variable adds a small layer of indirection that the rest of the function doesn't use.

findings.title: Inline comment on the `if not chunk: continue` line duplicates the block comment above
findings.detail: The block comment at lines 803–808 already explains the CRLF-splitting scenario thoroughly. The inline comment `# it's possible that after stripping the '\n' then chunk becomes empty` repeats what is already clear from the code itself, which is inconsistent with the file's convention of avoiding redundant inline comments on self-evident guards.

findings.title: `mock_iter_content` defined with `def` but the original used a `lambda` — inconsistency within the same test file
findings.detail: The surrounding untouched test code and the original version of these tests used `lambda` for one-liner mock callables. The change upgrades to `def` (which is fine for multi-line bodies), but the resulting function is still short and the file mixes both styles. The `def` form is actually more readable here given the conditional, so this is purely a nit about the now-inconsistent style across nearby tests that still use `lambda`.

findings.title: Line exceeds typical line length visible in the surrounding file
findings.detail: `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is quite long. The surrounding test file keeps lines reasonably short. Splitting the assertion into a separate `result = …` / `assert result == …` pair (as done on lines 1342 and 1346) would be consistent with the style used just a few lines later in the same function.

findings.title: Comment `# decode_unicode=True, output unicode strings` precedes the non-unicode block in the test
findings.detail: The comment order at lines 1394–1400 is `decode_unicode=True` first, then `decode_unicode=None`. In the `test_response_lines` function immediately above (lines 1331–1357) the order is consistently `decode_unicode=None` first, then `decode_unicode=True`. The reversal in `test_response_lines_parametrized` is a minor stylistic inconsistency within the same test class.

findings.title: No test for chunk that is exactly '\n' after a CR-ending chunk becoming empty after strip
findings.detail:
The branch at line 818 (`if not chunk: continue`) handles the case where stripping the leading '
' leaves an empty chunk. The new parametrized test case `[b'a', b'
', b'
b']` covers a multi-chunk sequence but does not isolate the scenario where the CR chunk is followed by a chunk that is *only* '
' (so the stripped chunk becomes empty AND there is no subsequent data). For example, chunks like `[b'a', b'
']` (CR ends last chunk, next chunk is solely '
') where the stream ends immediately after are not directly tested. The `continue` path is reached in the sequence `[b'a', b'
', b'
b']` only incidentally; there is no test asserting the output when the '
'-only chunk is the last chunk in the stream.

findings.title: No test covering iter_lines with decode_unicode=False and a custom non-CRLF delimiter
findings.detail: The new code computes `carriage_return` and `line_feed` based on `decode_unicode`. The parametrized tests only check `delimiter=b'\r\n'` for raw bytes. A non-CRLF byte delimiter (e.g. `b'|'`) with `decode_unicode=False` is not tested, which means the `carriage_return`/`line_feed` selection path for the non-splitlines branch is not fully exercised.

findings.title: No test for multiple consecutive CR+LF splits across more than two chunk boundaries
findings.detail: The fix tracks only `last_chunk_ends_with_cr`. A case where every chunk boundary has a split CRLF pair (e.g. `[b'a\r', b'\nb\r', b'\nc']`) is not present in the parametrized test matrix, leaving the cumulative correctness of `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` reset between iterations untested for longer sequences.

findings.title: No test for the `continue` path when the `\n`-only chunk is the last chunk in the stream
findings.detail:
The branch `if not chunk: continue` (line 818-819) is only exercised incidentally in the sequence `[b'a', b'
', b'
b']`. There is no test asserting correct output when the `
`-only chunk is the final chunk in the stream, e.g. `[b'a', b'
']`. Without this, the guard that prevents a trailing empty line when the stream ends after a lone `
` (completing a split CRLF) is untested in isolation.

findings.title: No test for multiple consecutive CR+LF splits across more than two chunk boundaries
findings.detail: The fix relies on `last_chunk_ends_with_cr` being correctly reset on every iteration. A sequence where every chunk boundary splits a CRLF pair — e.g. `[b'a\r', b'\nb\r', b'\nc']` — is absent from the parametrized test matrix. Adding such a case would give confidence that the flag is correctly threaded through longer streaming scenarios.

findings.title: No test for `iter_lines` with `decode_unicode=False` and a non-CRLF delimiter
findings.detail: The new code selects `carriage_return` and `line_feed` based on `decode_unicode`, but the parametrized tests only check `delimiter=b'\r\n'` for raw bytes. A non-CRLF byte delimiter (e.g. `b'|'`) with `decode_unicode=False` is not covered, leaving the `carriage_return`/`line_feed` selection path for the non-splitlines branch only partially exercised.

findings.title: Comment/block order for `decode_unicode=True` vs `None` is reversed relative to the sibling test function
findings.detail: In `test_response_lines` the `decode_unicode=None` block comes before `decode_unicode=True`. In `test_response_lines_parametrized` the order is reversed. Keeping the order consistent would make the two test functions easier to compare.

findings.title: Long assertion line should be split for consistency with surrounding style
findings.detail: The assertion `assert list(r.iter_lines(decode_unicode=True, delimiter=u'\r\n')) == unicode_mock_data.split(u'\r\n')` is notably longer than surrounding lines. A few lines later (lines 1342/1346) the same test function already splits result computation and assertion — applying the same pattern here would be consistent.

findings.title: Inline comment on `if not chunk: continue` duplicates the block comment above
findings.detail: The block comment at lines 803–808 already explains the CRLF-splitting scenario. The inline comment on the `if not chunk: continue` guard repeats what the code makes self-evident, which is inconsistent with the file's convention of avoiding redundant inline comments.

findings.title: Minor style: single-use variables and verbose constant names
findings.detail: Two small style observations: (1) `carriage_return` and `line_feed` are always set as a pair and a brief inline comment at the point of use could reduce their scope. (2) `skip_first_char` is assigned and checked on the immediately following line; inlining it as `if last_chunk_ends_with_cr and chunk.startswith(line_feed):` would match the file's general avoidance of single-use named temporaries. Both are very minor and do not affect correctness.

findings.title: Mixed use of `def` and `lambda` for mock callables in the same test file
findings.detail: `mock_iter_content` is now defined with `def` while nearby untouched tests use `lambda` for one-liner mocks. The `def` form is more readable for the conditional body here, so this is purely a consistency nit rather than a real issue.
